summary: My name is Leah Mahaney Herder, and I am a queer transracial adoptee born in 1996 in Changde, China. I was adopted by an American couple when I was just eleven months old and grew up in Ohio and Wisconsin. Throughout my life, I have explored my identity deeply, joining LGBTQ+ groups and attending events that helped me understand and embrace who I am. Studying at the University of Minnesota, I continue to connect with my roots and advocate for queer Asian communities.

Changde is the city in China where I was born in 1996, located in the Hunan province. Although I was adopted from there as an infant, I returned in 2013 and again after my freshman year of college to reconnect with my birth country. Visiting Changde was challenging because I felt foreign and disconnected, but it inspired me to learn more about my origins and culture. This city represents the beginning of my journey as one of China’s lost daughters.

Hunan is the province in the People's Republic of China where my birth city, Changde, is located. Although I was adopted as a baby and raised in the United States, learning about Hunan helped me understand my cultural background. My trips back to China, including to Hunan, were important steps in reconnecting with my heritage despite feeling very Americanized. This province is a key part of my identity as a queer transracial adoptee.

The People's Republic of China is where I was born in 1996 and from where I was adopted as an infant. Returning there in 2013 and during college was both difficult and meaningful, as I struggled with feeling disconnected but also motivated to reclaim my roots. China is central to my story as one of its lost daughters, and my experiences there have shaped my journey of identity and belonging. My adoption and later visits highlight the complex relationship I have with my birth country.

I was adopted by an American couple and raised in the United States, specifically in Ohio and Wisconsin. Growing up here, I navigated my identity as a queer transracial adoptee and became involved in LGBTQ+ activism and community groups. The U.S. is where I found my voice through organizations like my school's Gay–straight alliance and Proud Theater. It is also where I studied at the University of Minnesota and continue to live an authentic life.

Ohio was one of the states where my family lived after adopting me from China. We moved there with my older brother before eventually relocating to Middleton, Wisconsin. My time in Ohio was part of my early American upbringing, where I began to understand my identity as a transracial adoptee. It was a stepping stone in my journey before settling in Wisconsin for my formative years.

I spent twelve years of my life in Middleton, Wisconsin, after moving there from Ohio. It was in Wisconsin during middle and high school that I began questioning my identity and became involved in LGBTQ+ activism. I joined my school's Gay–straight alliance and participated in events like Day of Silence and Transgender Day of Remembrance. Wisconsin was also where I attended the Leadership Training Institute camp in 2014, which was pivotal in defining my queer identity.

I am currently a student at the University of Minnesota Twin Cities, studying political science and Mandarin Chinese. The university provided me with opportunities to deepen my connection to my birth culture, including a study abroad program in China after my freshman year. Attending this institution has been instrumental in my continued journey of self-discovery and cultural reconnection. It is where I am shaping my future while embracing my complex identity.

I joined my high school's Gay–straight alliance shortly after starting high school in Wisconsin. This group was my first step into LGBTQ+ activism and community involvement. Through the GSA, I participated in important events like Day of Silence and Transgender Day of Remembrance, which helped me explore and affirm my queer identity. The alliance was a supportive space where I began to find my voice and connect with others.

Day of Silence was one of the events I participated in through my school's Gay–straight alliance during high school in Wisconsin. It is a day dedicated to raising awareness about the silencing and erasure of LGBTQ+ people. Taking part in this event helped me feel connected to the broader queer community and motivated me to continue my activism. It was part of my early journey toward embracing and advocating for my identity.

I took part in Transgender Day of Remembrance during my high school years as a member of the Gay–straight alliance in Wisconsin. This event honors those lost to anti-transgender violence and raises awareness about the struggles faced by the trans community. Participating in this day was a meaningful part of my activism and helped me understand the importance of visibility and remembrance. It strengthened my commitment to fighting for LGBTQ+ rights.

In August 2014, I attended the Leadership Training Institute, a camp for high school LGBTQ+ activists in Wisconsin. This experience was monumental for me as it gave me the courage and knowledge to define my queer identity confidently. At LTI, I participated in a Queer Asian Caucus where I found a community that understood the intersection of my racial and queer identities. The camp was a turning point in my journey toward self-acceptance and activism.

During my senior year of high school, I joined Proud Theater, a queer theater group for LGBTQ+ youth and allies. This group gave me a platform to share my story in my own words, which was incredibly empowering and healing. Proud Theater helped me educate others about injustice and strengthened my voice as a queer activist. It was a vital part of my journey toward living authentically and advocating for my community.

I chose to study Mandarin Chinese at the University of Minnesota as a way to reconnect with my birth culture after my trips to China. Learning the language has been an important part of my efforts to understand where I come from and to bridge the gap between my American upbringing and Chinese heritage. Studying Mandarin has deepened my appreciation for my roots and supports my ongoing search for my birth family.
